metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Formalize the WS6 → WS7 deploy ordering in the plan: state that the `LLM-Review` bot must be confirmed operational (voting on test changes) before the live WS7 cutover apply is executed, and document what to do if WS7 must be applied first (e.g., temporarily disable the vote requirement).
findings.finding: The deploy ordering between WS6 (review-bot operational) and WS7 (live ruleset cutover) is noted only as `relates_to` but the plan does not specify a hard prerequisite or coexistence behavior if WS7 goes live before the `LLM-Review` bot is fully voting.
findings.location: Dependencies section; Acceptance Criteria (live-gated)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Prerequisites / deploy order' section: (1) Confirm WS6 bot is live and voting; (2) Run WS7 cutover apply. If WS7 must go first (access window constraint), document a temporary exemption path (e.g., set Gerrit submit rule to not require LLM-Review vote until bot is confirmed live).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a precondition to the live-gated AC (or a sequencing note in Dependencies) stating that WS6 must be confirmed operational (e.g., 'LLM-Review has cast at least one vote on a Gerrit change') before the Gerrit-only lock is applied to `navapbc/rebar` `main`.
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges 'the review-bot must be voting before Gerrit-only enforcement is meaningful' (Dependencies) but the live-gated AC does not require or verify WS6 is operational before activating the lock, creating a sequencing contradiction between the dependency statement and the AC.
findings.location: Dependencies: 'relates_to WS6 (the review-bot must be voting before Gerrit-only enforcement is meaningful)' vs. Acceptance Criteria / Live-gated (no WS6 precondition stated)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to live-gated AC: 'Precondition: confirm WS6 is operational (LLM-Review bot has voted on at least one open Gerrit change for navapbc/rebar) before applying the ruleset cutover.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare one path as canonical for state purposes: if Path B is used, add a `terraform import` step to bring the created rulesets into state before anyone runs `terraform apply`; if Path A is canonical, mark Path B as 'emergency fallback — must import afterward' with the exact import commands.
findings.finding: The plan offers two equivalent apply paths (Path A: Terraform; Path B: gh-api script), but does not specify what happens to Terraform state if Path B is executed: the script creates the two rulesets outside Terraform's state file, so a subsequent `terraform apply` (Path A) would see zero managed resources and attempt to create duplicates — resulting in either a conflict error or two competing rulesets with the same name.
findings.location: infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md — Apply > Path A and Path B; Acceptance Criteria 'no drift / no competing ruleset'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the runbook after the Path B section:
```bash
# If Path B was used, import rulesets into TF state to prevent drift:
terraform import github_repository_ruleset.main_lock <MAIN_ID>
terraform import github_repository_ruleset.tag_lock <TAG_ID>
# Then verify: terraform plan should show no changes
terraform plan
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider splitting the CONTRIBUTING.md doc authoring into a separate, independently-closable ticket from the live infrastructure cutover + verification, so neither can be marked done while masking the other's incomplete state.
findings.finding: The ticket joins two independent goals — (1) authoring the CONTRIBUTING.md contributor guide (a documentation task) and (2) executing the live infrastructure cutover + verification (an ops/infra task) — via a structural 'and'. These serve different personas (contributor vs. GitHub admin) and can succeed or fail independently; closing the doc half without the cutover half leaves the system in a state where the guide describes a flow that isn't yet enforced.
findings.location: ## What (items 1–3) / ## Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Create WS7a (CONTRIBUTING.md + cutover plan reconcile, closable now) and WS7b (live apply + proof, blocked on GitHub admin access). Reference WS7b from the README or CONTRIBUTING.md with a 'Note: enforcement is pending WS7b' until the live apply is complete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit precondition check to the Live-gated AC: confirm the Gerrit server at `rebar.solutions.navateam.com` is live and the `rebar-gerrit-replication` deploy key is registered before attempting the replication proof (the S6 apply-prove-rollback did not execute this step).
findings.finding: The Live-gated AC asserts 'a change submitted in Gerrit replicates to GitHub main' as proof of cutover, but the plan nowhere verifies that the Gerrit server (rebar.solutions.navateam.com) and its replication plugin are still live at WS7 cutover time. S6's apply-prove-rollback only proved the ruleset lock; the Gerrit→GitHub replication step (S6 verify step 6) was not executed during S6 and is not listed as a precondition in WS7.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Live-gated section: 'a change submitted in Gerrit replicates to GitHub main'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Live-gated ACs or Dependencies section: 'Prerequisite: confirm Gerrit server is operational (`ssh -p 29418 <you>@rebar.solutions.navateam.com gerrit version`) and the `rebar-gerrit-replication` write-enabled deploy key is present on navapbc/rebar (`gh api /repos/navapbc/rebar/keys --jq \'.[].title\'`) before applying the lock — the replication proof step cannot pass otherwise.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reverse the cutover ordering in `apply-mirror-lock.sh` (or add a note to the cutover plan): CREATE the new DeployKey-only lock rulesets first, VERIFY they are active (empirically or via the API), THEN remove `main-protection` — eliminating the window where `main` has zero protection.
findings.finding: The apply-mirror-lock.sh script (the live-apply path) deletes `main-protection` as its FIRST step (before creating the new lock rulesets), creating a window where `main` has zero branch protection. The plan presents this as a high-impact, high-stakes cutover but does not flag this unprotected-window gap or prescribe the safer create-first ordering.
findings.location: ## What — item 1 (cutover apply); infra/github/apply-mirror-lock.sh lines 67–119
findings.suggested_fix: In apply-mirror-lock.sh, move the `--delete-main-protection` block to after steps 3a and 3b (i.e., after both new rulesets are confirmed created). The script already prints the new ruleset IDs; add an explicit check that both exist before proceeding to delete the old one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the proof artifact format for the live-gated AC — e.g., 'paste the full GitHub API error response for the rejected push AND the GitHub commit URL showing Gerrit-submitted change on `main`' — so the criterion is deterministically evaluable.
findings.finding: The live-gated proof AC says 'proof recorded on this ticket' but does not specify what artifact constitutes acceptable proof, making the criterion subjectively evaluable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / Live-gated: 'Proof recorded on this ticket: a direct GitHub push / PR-merge to main is REJECTED, and a change submitted in Gerrit replicates to GitHub main'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Proof recorded on this ticket' with: 'Paste to this ticket: (a) the full `git push` error output or GitHub API 403/422 response for a direct-push attempt, and (b) the GitHub commit URL on `main` whose author is the `rebar-gerrit-replication` deploy key, from a change submitted via Gerrit.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan step (and a Doable-now AC) to audit existing developer-facing docs (README, setup guides, any onboarding material) for stale GitHub PR contribution references and either update them or add a redirect notice pointing to CONTRIBUTING.md.
findings.finding: The plan has no item or AC addressing existing developer-facing documentation (e.g., onboarding guides, setup docs, or any README sections) that describe or imply the old GitHub PR contribution workflow. After cutover those references will be stale/misleading, but no audit or update step is planned.
findings.location: What §2 (CONTRIBUTING.md); Scope (IN/OUT); Acceptance Criteria

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the CONTRIBUTING.md scope to include: (a) what to do if the LLM-Review bot does not vote within a reasonable time (escalation path or who to contact); (b) how to abandon a Gerrit change; (c) the error message and fix when pushing without a Change-Id (hook installation instructions).
findings.finding: The CONTRIBUTING.md scope does not cover non-happy-path scenarios a contributor will encounter: (1) what to do when the LLM-Review bot is unavailable or stuck (no vote arrives, blocking submit indefinitely); (2) how to abandon a Gerrit change; (3) what error to expect and how to recover if a push to refs/for/main is rejected due to a missing Change-Id hook.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Doable now' §1 / 'What' §2 CONTRIBUTING.md scope

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact 'WS6 message tags' vocabulary (the string patterns / structured fields in the Gerrit robot-comment message that distinguish a real-finding `-1` from a `coverage-gap` infra veto), either by consulting the WS6 adapter spec or by confirming the prose patterns in `adapter.py _summarize()` that CONTRIBUTING.md should direct contributors to look for.
findings.finding: The CONTRIBUTING.md AC references 'the WS6 message tags' as the named vocabulary contributors use to distinguish a real-finding `-1` from a `coverage-gap` infra veto, but this vocabulary is never defined in the plan and is not confirmed to exist as structured, contributor-visible tags in the shipped or planned robot-comment message format.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Doable now: 'how to read a `-1` (a real finding vs a `coverage-gap` infra veto — the WS6 message tags)'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) confirm that the distinguishing cues are prose-level (e.g. 'rebar code review unavailable' vs 'rebar code review: N finding(s)') and document those exact strings in the plan's CONTRIBUTING.md outline, or (b) if WS6's adapter re-implementation adds explicit tag prefixes (e.g. `[coverage-gap]` vs `[finding]`), reference those concrete strings in the AC so the CONTRIBUTING.md author has a spec to write from.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before the live cutover, enumerate all non-human writers to `navapbc/rebar` `main` (GitHub Actions workflows, bots, Dependabot) and confirm each is either already using the replication deploy key path or has a documented migration/disable plan.
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges that developers 'still open GitHub PRs' as the old behavior being replaced, but does not enumerate or plan for other automated callers (e.g., GitHub Actions workflows that push to main, bots, or CI release automation) whose operations will silently start failing once the DeployKey-only lock is active.
findings.location: 'Why' section / 'What' §1 / Scope — IN

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the complete expected set of active rulesets/branch-protections on `main` after cutover (e.g., 'exactly one ruleset — the S6 DeployKey-only ruleset; no other rulesets, classic branch protections, or required status checks') so the executor has a deterministic done-state rather than open-ended discovery.
findings.finding: The plan states 'any other human/PR-merge path is removed/superseded' without enumerating what those other paths are, leaving an open-ended discovery task for the executor.
findings.location: What, item 1: 'Ensure the pre-existing main-protection ruleset (18048287) and any other human/PR-merge path is removed/superseded'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the cutover plan AC: 'Confirmed via GitHub API (`GET /repos/navapbc/rebar/rulesets`) that exactly one ruleset governs `main` (the S6 DeployKey-only ruleset) and no classic branch protection rules exist on `main`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a GitHub Audit Log verification step to the runbook (e.g., `gh api '/orgs/navapbc/audit-log?phrase=repo.create_actions_secret&per_page=5'` or the equivalent ruleset event) to confirm the ruleset creation is attributed to the expected actor.
findings.finding: The verification steps after apply focus entirely on empirical push/reject tests but do not include a check of the GitHub Audit Log to confirm the ruleset creation event is attributable to the expected principal. For a security-posture change that makes `main` write-locked, audit-log confirmation is the observability signal that the change was applied by the right actor and not by an unexpected party.
findings.location: infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md — Verify empirically (lines 91-111)
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the Verify section:
```bash
# 7. Confirm via audit log that the ruleset was created by the expected actor:
gh api '/orgs/navapbc/audit-log?phrase=protected_branch&per_page=10' \
  --jq '.[] | {action, actor, created_at, repo}'
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the Path A runbook commands, replace `terraform plan` + `terraform apply` with `terraform plan -out mirror-lock.tfplan` + `terraform apply mirror-lock.tfplan` to make the reviewed plan and the applied plan atomic.
findings.finding: The runbook's Path A shows `terraform plan` and `terraform apply` as two separate bare commands without saving the plan to a file (`-out plan.tfplan` / `terraform apply plan.tfplan`). For this high-impact, repo-locking operation, a concurrent API change between the plan and apply steps could cause Terraform to apply a different plan than the one reviewed — defeating the plan-before-apply safety discipline.
findings.location: infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md — Apply > Path A (lines 60-65)
findings.suggested_fix:
```bash
terraform plan -out mirror-lock.tfplan   # review this output
terraform apply mirror-lock.tfplan       # applies exactly what was reviewed
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align terminology: pick one canonical name for the post-cutover ruleset (e.g., 'Gerrit-only / DeployKey-bypass ruleset') and use it consistently in AC2, AC3, and the plan body.
findings.finding: AC2 (Doable now) calls the sole remaining ruleset the 'S6 DeployKey-only ruleset' while AC3 (Live-gated) calls the same entity the 'Gerrit-only ruleset'; the plan body also uses both terms interchangeably. A reviewer verifying AC2 and AC3 must infer they refer to the same object.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (Doable now) and AC3 (Live-gated)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'S6 DeployKey-only ruleset' in AC2 with 'Gerrit-only ruleset' (or vice-versa) so both ACs use the same label.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Live-gated AC (or extend AC3) requiring that the rollback runbook and `main-protection.snapshot.json` are confirmed accurate after WS7 reconciliation — i.e., a rollback dry-run or explicit validation step is documented as part of the cutover.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion verifies that the rollback path (the existing `github-mirror-lock.md` runbook and `main-protection.snapshot.json`) remains valid and operable AFTER WS7's reconciliation work. If WS7 reconciles or modifies the ruleset configuration during the 'no drift' reconciliation step, the pre-existing snapshot may become stale, leaving operators with a broken rollback.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Doable-now AC2 / Live-gated AC3; Context section referencing `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md` and `infra/github/main-protection.snapshot.json`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a deliverable (ADR or `docs/architecture/` entry or `CLAUDE.md` update) that formally records the decision to make GitHub a read-only mirror gated by Gerrit, including the rationale and the consequence that direct pushes/PR merges are permanently rejected.
findings.finding: The architectural decision to make GitHub a read-only mirror (sole access via Gerrit deploy-key replication) is not captured in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update — only in a developer-facing CONTRIBUTING.md and an operational runbook.
findings.location: What / Scope sections; no ADR or CLAUDE.md update is mentioned anywhere in the plan
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0007-github-read-only-mirror.md` (or equivalent) with: Context (legacy PR flow), Decision (Gerrit-only via deploy-key ruleset), Status (accepted), Consequences (all changes must go through Gerrit; direct pushes rejected). Link from CONTRIBUTING.md and README.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the S6 DeployKey-only ruleset is confirmed active (e.g., verified via GitHub API) BEFORE the legacy `main-protection` ruleset is removed, or document the two steps as a single atomic Terraform apply to eliminate the unprotected window.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the transition window between removing the legacy `main-protection` ruleset and confirming the S6 DeployKey-only ruleset is the sole active protection — during this gap `main` could be momentarily unprotected and accept direct pushes or PR merges.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Live-gated / 'What' §1 'Remove the legacy protection + activate the Gerrit-only ruleset'
findings.suggested_fix: In the cutover runbook / AC, add a prerequisite step: verify the S6 ruleset is enabled and applied to `main` (GET /repos/navapbc/rebar/rulesets/{id}) before issuing the DELETE on ruleset 18048287; or combine both operations into a single `terraform apply` that adds the new resource and removes the old in one API transaction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note in the cutover plan confirming that the Terraform apply is idempotent (Terraform declarative plan already handles this) and specify what a safe manual re-run looks like if the first attempt partially fails.
findings.finding: The plan does not address idempotency of the cutover apply — if `terraform apply` or the removal script is run a second time (e.g., after drift or a failed first attempt), the outcome is unspecified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Live-gated / 'What' §1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to the cutover plan (and CONTRIBUTING.md or runbook reference) explaining WHY `main-protection` must be removed — not just that it should be: GitHub rulesets are cumulative, and its `required_status_checks` rule would reject replication commits (which carry no CI run) even though those commits bypass the new DeployKey-only lock.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for WHY removing `main-protection` entirely (rather than leaving it co-active) is mechanistically necessary — specifically that `main-protection`'s `required_status_checks` rule would block replication pushes (which have no associated GitHub Actions run and can never satisfy those CI contexts). Without this rationale, an implementer might treat removal as optional cleanup and leave `main-protection` active, silently breaking replication.
findings.location: ## What — item 1; ## Acceptance Criteria — 'names the exact legacy ruleset(s) to remove'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
